problem: The Leaning Tower of Pisa is 55 m tall and about 7.7 m radius. The top is 4.5 m off-center. How much farther can it lean before the tower has to fall?
solution: Once the center of mass tips past the bottom base of the tower, it will tip. If the center of mass is past 7.7 m off center, then the top will be two times that; 15.4 m off center. Thus the tower can lean 11.1 m extra at the top before it tips.

For the force on the left, we can do torque balance about the right beam. Here we have $$F_L\ell =Mg\frac{\ell}{2}+\frac 12 Mg\frac{3\ell}{4}\Rightarrow F_L=\frac 78 Mg$$For the force on the right, we can do torque balance about the left beam. Here we have $$F_R\ell =Mg\frac{\ell}{2}+\frac 12 Mg\frac{\ell}{4}\Rightarrow F_L=\frac 58 Mg$$

For the force on the left, we can do torque balance about the right beam. Here we have $$F_A\cdot 10=4300\cdot 8+3100\cdot 4+2200\cdot 1+280\cdot 9.8\cdot 5\Rightarrow F_L=6272\, \unit N$$
    For the force on the right, we can do torque balance about the left beam. Here we have $$F_B\cdot 10=4300\cdot 2+3100\cdot 6+2200\cdot 9+280\cdot 9.8\cdot 5\Rightarrow F_R=6072\, \unit N$$

problem: A person stands, hands at his side, on a platform that is rotating at a rate of 0.90 rev/s. If he raises his arms to a horizontal position, the speed of rotation decreases to 0.60 rev/s. (a) Why? (b) By what factor has his moment of inertia changed?

(a) What is the angular momentum of a figure skater spinning at 3.0 rev/s with arms in close to her body, assuming her to be a uniform cylinder with a height of 1.5 m, a radius of 15 cm, and a mass of 48 kg? (b) How much torque is required to slow her to a stop in 4.0 s, assuming she does not move her arms?

A potter’s wheel is rotating around a vertical axis through its center at a frequency of 1.5 rev/s. The wheel can be considered a uniform disk of mass 5.0 kg and diameter 0.40 m. The potter then throws a 2.6-kg chunk of clay, approximately shaped as a flat disk of radius 7.0 cm, onto the center of the rotating wheel. What is the frequency of the wheel after the clay sticks to it? Ignore friction.
solution: Let the initial and final frequencies be $f$ and $f'$. The angular momentum is conserved, so we have $$\frac 12 MR^2f=\left(\frac 12 MR^2+\frac12 mr^2\right)f'$$ so rearranging yields $$f'=\frac{MR^2}{MR^2+mr^2}f=\frac{5\cdot 0.2^2}{5\cdot 0.2^2+2.6\cdot 0.07^2}1.5=1.41\, \unit{rev/s}$$

problem: Calculate the forces $F_A$ and $F_B$ that the supports exert on the diving board of the previous problem when a 52-kg person stands at its tip. (a) Ignore the weight of the board. (b) Now take into account the board’s mass of 28 kg. Assume the board is uniform.
What is the angular momentum of a 0.270-kg ball revolving on the end of a thin string in a circle of radius 1.35 m at an angular speed of 10.4 rad/s?

solution: Let the tension in the left wire be $T_1$ and in the right $T_2$. By force balance in the $x$ and $y$ directions, $$T_1\sin(53)+T_2\sin(37)=33\cdot 9.8\qquad T_1\cos(53)=T_2\cos(37)$$and solving the system we get $$T_1=258\, \unit N\qquad T_2=195\, \unit N$$

We only need to find the horizontal tension exerted by the ropes, because that is the component balanced out by the net (the pole will balance out the vertical forces). By some similar triangles and trig, the force is just $$T=2\cdot 115\cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{2^2+2.6^2}}\cdot \cos(30)=121.4\, \unit N$$
Let the masses of the objects be $M$. By conservation of angular momentum, $$\frac 12 MR^2f=\left(\frac 12 MR^2+\frac 1{12}M(2R)^2\right)f'\Rightarrow f'=\frac{1/2}{1/2+1/3}f=1.98\, \unit{rev/s}$$

problem: An asteroid of mass $1.0 \times 10^5$ kg, traveling at a speed of 35 km/s relative to the Earth, hits the Earth at the equator tangentially, in the direction of Earth’s rotation, and is embedded there. Use angular momentum to estimate the percent change in the angular speed of the Earth as a result of the collision. (You can search up the radius and mass of the Earth and assume it is a uniform sphere.)
solution: A day is 24 hours, which is 86400 seconds. The frequency of revolution is then $f=1/86400=1.16\times 10^{-5}\, \unit{rev/s}$, and the angular velocity is $\omega_0 = 2\pi f=7.29\times 10^{-5}\, \unit{rad/s}$. The moment of inertia of the Earth, using $M=5.97\times 10^{26}\, \unit{kg}$ and $R=6.37\times 10^{6} \, \unit{m}$, is then $I_E=(2/5)MR^2=(2/5)\cdot 5.97\times 10^{26}\cdot (6.37\times 10^{6})^2=9.69\times 10^{39}\, \unit{kg\cdot m^2}.$ The initial angular momentum of the asteroid is $mvR=1.0\times 10^5\cdot 35000\cdot 6.37\times 10^{6}=2.23\times 10^{16}\, \unit{kg \cdot m^2/s}$. The difference in angular velocity this additional momentum imparts on the Earth (we can neglect the inertia of the embedded asteroid because it is so small) is $L/I_E=2.23\times 10^{16}/9.69\times 10^{39}=2.30\times 10^{-24}\, \unit{rad/s}$ which is a fraction $2.30\times 10^{-24}/7.29\times 10^{-5}=3.16\times 10^{-20}$ of the original angular velocity. This is such a small amount!
